title: How to Roll — The Core Mechanic
body:
Roll *d100* (two ten-sided dice read as a percentage, 00–99) under your relevant Stat or Save to succeed.

• *Advantage [+]:* Roll twice, take the lower result.
• *Disadvantage [-]:* Roll twice, take the higher result.
• *90–99 always fails*, even with Advantage.
• *Critical Success:* matching doubles that fall under your stat (e.g. 33 when stat is 45).
• *Critical Failure:* matching doubles that fall over your stat (e.g. 77 when stat is 45).

title: The Four Stats
body:
Roll *2d10+25* for each of the four stats at character creation. Average result: 36.

• *Strength* — Physical feats, melee damage base (Str ÷ 10, round down).
• *Speed* — Initiative order, evasion, chases.
• *Intellect* — Knowledge, problem-solving, technology.
• *Combat* — Accuracy when attacking with weapons.

title: The Three Saves
body:
Roll *2d10+10* for each save at character creation.

• *Sanity* — Resists psychological horror, alien incomprehensibility, madness.
• *Fear* — Resists immediate dread, panic triggers, terrifying revelations. Also used for Panic Checks.
• *Body* — Resists physical trauma, toxins, decompression, disease.

title: Character Creation — 9 Steps
body:
1. *Roll Stats* — 2d10+25 each for Strength, Speed, Intellect, Combat.
2. *Roll Saves* — 2d10+10 each for Sanity, Fear, Body.
3. *Choose Class* — Apply class stat/save bonuses.
4. *Roll Health* — 1d10+10 = Maximum Health. Start at Max with 0 Wounds.
5. *Gain Stress* — Current Stress and Minimum Stress both start at 2.
6. *Note Trauma Response* — Mark your class Trauma Response.
7. *Choose Skills* — Each class has preloaded skills plus bonus picks.
8. *Roll Loadout, Trinket & Patch* — d10 on class table; d100 for Trinket and Patch.
9. *Finishing* — Write name and pronouns. Mark 0 above High Score. Ready to play.

title: Starting Credits
body:
Roll *2d10×10cr* in addition to your Starting Loadout.

If you forgo your Loadout entirely, roll *2d10×100cr* instead.

Credits are tight — you are working-class spacers, not heroes.

title: Initiative & Turn Order
body:
*Rounds* — Combat runs in ~10 second intervals. Everything in a round happens at roughly the same time.

*Each Round:*
1. Warden describes the situation and likely outcome if no one acts.
2. Players describe how their characters react.
3. Everyone commits to a course of action.
4. Warden resolves all actions simultaneously, calling for any Stat Checks or Saves.
5. Everyone rolls. Damage and Wounds are rolled after.
6. Warden describes the new situation. Next round begins.

*Strict Turn Order (optional):* At the start of an encounter, everyone makes a *Speed Check*. Those who succeed act before enemy hostiles; those who fail act after. If the situation changes dramatically, Speed Checks can be called again.

*Surprise:* If characters may be ambushed or shocked, the Warden calls for a *Fear Save*. Those who succeed can react. Those who fail are too shocked to act until the next round.

title: Making an Attack
body:
On your turn, make a *Combat Check*: roll d100 under your Combat stat.

• *Success* → roll weapon damage.
• *Failure* → the attack misses.
• *Critical Success* (matching doubles under) → something exceptional happens.
• *Critical Failure* (matching doubles over) → something bad happens (weapon jams, friendly fire, etc.).
• *(AA) Auto-Attack weapons* hit automatically — no Combat Check needed, just roll damage.

title: Damage & Armor Points
body:
After a successful hit:

1. Roll the weapon's damage dice.
2. Subtract the target's *AP (Armor Points)*.
3. Remaining damage is dealt to *Health*.

AP regenerates between encounters. Advanced Battle Dress also applies *Damage Reduction 3* after AP (subtract another 3 from the remaining damage).

title: Taking a Wound
body:
When Health drops to *0*, take 1 Wound:
1. Roll 1d10 on the *Wound Table* for the damage type (Blunt Force, Bleeding, Gunshot, Fire/Explosives, or Gore).
2. Reset Health to Maximum minus any carryover damage.

If Wounds exceed *Max Wounds* (base 2; Marines & Androids: 3) → you die.

*Severity guide:*
• *Flesh Wound* — Minor inconvenience.
• *Minor Injury* — Lasting issue; needs medical attention.
• *Major Injury* — Serious; requires surgery.
• *Lethal Injury* — Death Save in 10d10 rounds without treatment.
• *Fatal Injury* — Death Save immediately.

*Bleeding +N* — Each round without treatment, ongoing damage. Stop with First Aid Kit or Field Medicine.

Some attacks deal Wounds directly, bypassing Armor and Damage Reduction.

title: Wound Types
body:
Each weapon deals a specific wound type on the Wound Table:

• *Bleeding* — Scalpel, Hand Welder, Rigging Gun, Laser Cutter, Vibechete
• *Gore* — Boarding Axe, Laser Cutter [+], Vibechete
• *Gunshot* — Combat Shotgun, Revolver, Pulse Rifle, SMG, Smart Rifle, GPMG
• *Blunt Force* — Crowbar, Foam Gun, Stun Baton, Tranq Pistol, Unarmed
• *Fire/Explosives* — Flamethrower, Flare Gun, Frag Grenade

Some weapons specify [+] or [-] on the wound roll. Vibechete causes a Wound on *both* Bleeding AND Gore columns simultaneously.

title: Stress — How It Works
body:
Stress is the core tension engine of Mothership.

• *Current Stress* starts at 2.
• *Minimum Stress* starts at 2 — it cannot go lower.
• Stress *increases* from horror, failed saves, and bad events.
• Whenever Current Stress *equals or exceeds* Minimum Stress, make a *Panic Check* (Fear Save).
• On a failed Panic Check, roll on the *Panic Table*.
• Stress *decreases* during Shore Leave between missions.

title: Panic Check
body:
*What is a Panic Check?*
Roll the Panic Die (1d20) and attempt to roll *greater than your current Stress.* If you roll equal to or less than your Stress, you fail and roll on the Panic Table.

*When to make Panic Checks:*
• On a Critical Failure on any Stat Check or Save.
• Watching another crewmember die.
• Witnessing more than 1 crewmember Panic simultaneously.
• Ship rolls a Critical Failure — everyone on board checks.
• Encountering a strange or horrifying entity for the first time.
• When all hope is lost and death feels certain.
• Whenever you, the player, want.

*Conditions*
Some Panic Table results leave lasting impressions called *Conditions* — they affect you until treated. See Medical Care for treatment options.

title: Trauma Responses by Class
body:
Each class has a Trauma Response that cascades panic to nearby crew:

• *Marine* — When you panic, every nearby friendly crew must make a Fear Save.
• *Android* — Fear Saves made by nearby crew are at Disadvantage [-].
• *Scientist* — When you fail a Sanity Save, all nearby crew gain 1 Stress.
• *Teamster* — Once per session, may take Advantage [+] on one Panic Check (beneficial, not harmful).

title: Skill Tiers
body:
Skills have three tiers. You must take the prerequisite tier first:

• *Trained (+10)* — Basic competency. Adds +10 to the relevant stat check.
• *Expert (+15)* — Advanced competency. Adds +15. Requires Trained in the tree.
• *Master (+20)* — Specialist. Adds +20. Requires Expert in the tree.

Skill bonuses stack with stat values. You can only add one Skill Bonus to a Ship Stat Check at a time.

title: Skill Trees
body:
Three tiers: *Trained* (+10), *Expert* (+15), *Master* (+20). Any listed prerequisite is sufficient.

*Trained → Expert*
Linguistics or Zoology → Psychology
Zoology or Botany → Pathology
Zoology → Field Medicine
Botany or Geology → Ecology
Botany → Wilderness Survival
Geology or Industrial Equipment → Asteroid Mining
Industrial Equipment or Jury-Rigging → Mechanical Repair
Jury-Rigging or Chemistry or Military Training → Explosives
Chemistry → Pharmacology
Computers → Hacking
Zero-G → Piloting
Mathematics → Physics
Art or Archaeology or Theology → Mysticism
Military Training or Rimwise → Firearms
Military Training or Rimwise or Athletics → Hand-to-Hand Combat

*Expert → Master*
Psychology → Sophontology
Pathology → Exobiology
Pathology or Field Medicine → Surgery
Ecology or Asteroid Mining → Planetology
Mechanical Repair → Robotics, Engineering, Cybernetics
Hacking → Artificial Intelligence
Piloting or Physics or Mysticism → Hyperspace
Piloting or Firearms → Command
Mysticism → Xenoesotericism

title: Class Starting Skills
body:
Each class starts with preloaded skills and additional picks:

• *Marine* — Preloaded: Military Training, Athletics. Bonus: 1 Expert Skill OR 2 Trained Skills.
• *Android* — Preloaded: Linguistics, Computers, Mathematics. Bonus: 1 Expert Skill OR 2 Trained Skills.
• *Scientist* — Preloaded: 1 Master Skill + 1 Expert + 1 Trained. Bonus: 1 Trained Skill.
• *Teamster* — Preloaded: Industrial Equipment, Zero-G. Bonus: 1 Trained + 1 Expert Skill.

title: Ship Stat Checks
body:
Ships have three stats that function as both stats and saves:

• *Thrusters* — Maneuvering and acceleration. Like Speed.
• *Weapons* — Targeting and attacking other ships. Like Combat.
• *Systems* — Sensors, computers, maintenance, other subsystems.

Whenever a ship *fails* a Stat Check, *everyone aboard gains 1 Stress*.
90–99 always fails. Critical Failure = everyone aboard makes a Panic Check.

One crew member may add their relevant Skill Bonus to a Ship Stat Check at a time.

title: Ship Combat
body:
Ship combat follows the same structure as personal combat:

1. *Initiative* — Highest Thrusters acts first.
2. *Attack* — Roll d100 under Weapons stat.
3. *Damage* — Ship weapons deal Megadamage (MDMG) dice.
4. *Critical Hits* — Roll on the Status Report (Megadamage Track).
5. *Failed Ship Saves* — Everyone aboard gains 1 Stress.
6. *Critical Failure* — Everyone makes a Panic Check.
7. *Escape* — Thrusters Check to disengage.

title: Transponder
body:
Every ship broadcasts an automated Transponder signal containing: Ship Identifier/name, make/model, jump drive info, ship class and type.

*Illegal (or highly suspect) to turn off* in most star systems. Disabling a transponder is a red flag to authorities and other ships.

title: The TOMBS Cycle
body:
Every Horror in Mothership follows a 5-act structure called the *TOMBS Cycle* — *Transgression, Omens, Manifestation, Banishment,* and *Slumber.*

💡 *Prepping your Horror*
Write down the Acts on your Session 01 Prep page, then use the guidelines to invent your Horror. Use the Random Horrors Tables to generate ideas — the table is only a starting point. Breathe life into the entries, ignore what you don't like, and combine unrelated concepts to create something unique.

*ACT I — TRANSGRESSION*
*Someone knowingly or unknowingly awakens the Horror from its slumber.*

A line was crossed that wasn't meant to be. It may be a physical boundary — boarding a derelict ship, landing on an unknown planet — or a moral one, like leaving a crewmember behind or turning your back on an innocent's suffering. Transgressions can be past events that explain why the Horror exists at all, or present actions the players are trying to stop. Players will usually only realize they have Transgressed by the time it's too late and they enter the next Act: *Omens.*

*ACT II — OMENS*
*The Horror heralds its arrival through signs and signals.*

Omens take the form of nightmares, a trail of victims, or whispers among colonists — an early warning that something is wrong and a chance to get ahead of the Horror. They build tension and act as mystery clues. For looming long-term threats, Omens may be spaced months apart. For impending terror, an Omen may simply be a corpse with its chest split open. Once solved — or time runs out — the Horror appears: *Manifestation.*

*ACT III — MANIFESTATION*
*The Horror reveals its true form to the players.*

The spirit appears, the serial killer attacks, the disease infects, the monster rampages. The Manifestation gives players a form, body, or face to fight. Think hard about its appearance — its smells, the sounds it makes creeping up on you. Remember: a Manifestation is a symptom. Once Manifested, players are on the defense — hunted, surviving long enough to reach: *Banishment.*

*ACT IV — BANISHMENT*
*The players race against the clock to fight back and destroy the Horror.*

Banishment can mean simply killing the Horror — but not always. It may need to be appeased, or freed from its torment. Attacks increase, viruses spread, deaths multiply. Whether the players Banish the Horror, run away, or die trying, the Horror enters its final phase: *Slumber.*

*ACT V — SLUMBER*
*The Horror relents… for now.*

If Banished, the Horror goes dormant — waiting for the next Transgression. If the players fail, it completes its plan and moves on to the next phase of its evolution. The Horror is rarely defeated permanently, only kept at bay for another day. There is always work to do. The long-term scars of its existence linger — and always, the Cycle starts again.

title: Session Prep — 10 Steps
body:
1. Get organized — label notebook: Index, Roster, Session Prep.
2. Invite 3–4 friends. Schedule 3–4 hours.
3. Read the Player's Survival Guide.
4. Choose a Starting Scenario.
5. Note the Setting (roll d10 or choose).
6. Find the Horror — use the Random Horrors Table.
7. Create a Survival Obstacle.
8. Something to Solve / Someone to Save.
9. Map it out with shorthand notes.
10. Find hidden connections, discard weak ideas.

title: The Three Pillars — Survive, Solve, Save
body:
Players can usually only accomplish one, maybe two:

• *Survive the Nightmare* — Outlast the Horror, escape with your lives.
• *Solve the Mystery* — Understand what is happening and why.
• *Save the Day* — Rescue someone, stop the Horror, protect others.

Design scenarios with hard problems and escalating threats. A good story only happens in retrospect — design *situations*, not plots.

title: Obstacle Types
body:
Five categories of obstacles for scenarios:

• *Psychological Trauma* — Creepy environments, isolation, evidence of violence, Omens.
• *Violent Encounters* — Combat. Use sparingly and brutally.
• *Environmental Hazards* — Toxic atmosphere, radiation, zero-G, lack of O2.
• *Social Pressures* — Negotiation, factions, allies, enemies.
• *Resource Scarcity* — O2, food, ammo, fuel, Warp Cores, credits.

title: ABH — Situation Overview
body:
*Planet:* Samsa VI — colonized world with active terraforming.

*The Signal:* Hinton (synthetic science officer) discovered the carcinid Shriek frequency and engineered it into a Signal that expands carcinid reproduction. He broadcasts it from the Orbital Relay, jamming all radio communications on Samsa VI. Anyone attempting radio contact risks Shriek infection.

*Goal:* Discover what's happening, stop the Signal, survive.

*Key locations:* Greta Base (pg.10), Heron Terraforming Station (pg.16), The Carcinid Mothership (pg.30).

*Recommended play:* Scenario 1 → 2 → 3 → 4, but players drive the path.

title: ABH — The Carcinids
body:
*Giant arthropod-insectoid creatures.* Natural weapons and near-impenetrable carapace.

*Carapace:* Bullet and flameproof Aramid Fiber Weave — AP 30. *Weakness:* Hydrofluoric acid.

*The Shriek:* Their primary horror weapon. Triggers a Sanity Save. On failure: +1 Stress and Infection begins (see ABH Rules — Infection Stages).

*Stat block:* Combat 75 / Instinct 75 / Claw 4d10 DMG / Wounds 2 (20 HP) / AP 30.

title: ABH — Infection Stages
body:
Characters exposed to the Shriek who fail a Sanity Save become Infected. Infection progresses in stages:

• *Stage 1:* Recurring headaches, strange compulsions, slight personality shifts.
• *Stage 2:* Hearing the Shriek even in silence, acting in the carcinids' interest without realising.
• *Stage 3:* Full carcinid psychic possession. Character becomes an NPC hostile to the crew.

Radio contact on Samsa VI while the Signal broadcasts: Sanity Save or begin Stage 1.

title: ABH — Stopping the Signal
body:
Two actions required to stop the Signal:

1. *Remove the carc from the controls* in the Control Room [4] at Heron Station — one round action.
2. *Manually reset the Orbital Relay [5]* on the roof — one round action.

Once both are done, radio communications return. Disoriented carcinids retreat: Chimney [11] → Tunnels [15] → The Mothership.

*Roof hazard:* Heavy rain, slippery surface. Falls are lethal.

title: DP — Dead Planet Overview
body:
*Premise:* Somewhere in the universe there is a planet with a dark energy that sucks ships out of hyperspace and strands them in orbit. Jump drives don't work here — only thrusters, which would take hundreds of years to escape.

*The Gaunt:* Nightmare-inducing parasites from a Dead Dimension. Their goal: escape the Dead Planet and flood the universe with necrosis. They do this by compelling captive species to build and open the Dead Gateway.

*Ways to win:* De-power the Dead Gateway, destroy it, blow up the planet, or hitch a ride with Brekt (6 seats).

title: DP — Nightmare Check Rules
body:
A *Sanity Save* made when resting. Frequency depends on location:

• *On the Moon (orbit):* Once a week.
• *On Tyrant Beggar Moon Colony:* Every other night.
• *On the Dead Planet surface:* Every night.

*Failure:* Gain 1 Stress and roll a result from the Nightmare Table.
*Nightmares prevent healing* and the benefits of rest.
*Same nightmare twice:* 1d10 Stress; it becomes your only recurring dream.
*Three failures in a row:* Permanent Night Terrors — gain 1d10[+] Stress every nightmare.

title: DP — Compulsion Mechanic
body:
After two or more failed Nightmare Checks while significantly exposed to the Gaunt:

1. Wake up with a *Compulsion to sculpt* a small terrifying object.
2. Roll on the *Compulsive Nightmarish Sculpture Table* (d10).
3. Have *1d10 days* to complete it. Each day you don't: gain 1d10 Stress.
4. After completing, future Nightmare Checks: take no Stress but must build another, OR take Stress equal to number of sculptures already made.
5. Each sculpture created: Warden rolls d100. If result ≤ total sculptures held by crew → *Gaunt gate opens*.
6. Losing/destroying a sculpture: 1d10 permanent damage to Sanity Save.

title: GD — Gradient Descent Overview
body:
*Location:* THE DEEP — formerly CLOUDBANK Synthetics Production Facility, now controlled by the rogue AI *Monarch*. A megadungeon android factory in rimspace, surrounded by a corporate blockade of Troubleshooters.

*Theme:* Identity horror. Are you who you think you are? Are androids people?

*The Bends mechanic:* Characters may secretly be Infiltrator Androids with implanted human memories. Discuss with all players before using.

*Monarch:* Not simply hostile — alien motivations. It *is* the building. It gains Stress when systems are damaged.

title: GD — The Bends
body:
The Bends measures how certain a character is of their own identity. It operates like Stress but targets identity rather than sanity.

As The Bends increases, a character may:
• Doubt their own memories.
• Act against the group's interests.
• Ultimately *refuse to leave THE DEEP* — becoming an NPC permanently.

*Important:* Talk to all players before introducing Infiltrator Androids or The Bends mechanic. It can be deeply unsettling and is not for every table.

title: APF — Prospero's Dream Overview
body:
*Prospero's Dream* is a massive space station with 8,000,000+ inhabitants. Overcrowded, haphazard, criminal.

*Controlled by:* Golyonova II Bratva (The Novos) — a criminal syndicate run by the secret android *Yandee*.
*Security:* Tempest Mercenary Company.
*Primary economy:* Sycorax drug trade (supplied by Solarian Church, distributed by Teamsters Local 32819L).

*On arrival:* Ships with Hull ≤70 enter dry dock. Hull 71+ moor at piers. All ships need a current manifest. All crew receive an *O2 Credstick* after passing through the Clean Room.

title: APF — Station Laws
body:
*The Laws of The Dream:*

• Have your O2 Credstick at all times. Movements tracked.
• *Contraband (banned):* Laser Cutters, Explosives, Signal Jammers, Contagious Bioweapons, EMP tech.
• Non-hull-piercing weapons permitted. Hull-breaching weapons go in a *rented locker* (1cr/day/weapon).
• Don't enter unauthorized areas, especially The Choke.
• Street justice is pervasive. Major breaches handled by Tempest Co. and The Court.

*Oxygen Tax:* 10cr/day/person. 50cr/day during major events.

title: APF — The Court
body:
Prospero's Dream's legal system: *trial by combat*.

Those who can't pay fees or are arrested appear before Chief Adjudicator *Brunhildh*. Fight in *The Cage* (electrified, revolving stage — Body Save or 1d10 DMG and lose an action).

*Losing* = death or *The Choke* (O2 debtors' prison).
*Winning* = charges dropped.

Power brokers watch from *The Bench:* Angus (Databroker), Reidmar (Teamster Rep.), Cmdr. Cutter (Tempest Co.), Yandee.

title: ST — Space Travel & Navigation
body:
• Ships use *Warp Cores* to jump through hyperspace between systems.
• *Jump Rating* determines how far per Warp Core consumed.
• Navigation checks use the *Systems* stat.
• Ships spend Fuel each month of travel.
• *Failed navigation:* 10% chance all navigation data wiped.
• Cryosleep is standard for long journeys.

Without Warp Cores, sub-light travel between systems takes *decades*.

title: Range Bands
body:
All weapons have a maximum effective range band. Attacks beyond that range are at the Warden's discretion — usually impossible or heavily penalised.

*Adjacent* — Arm's reach (melee). Unarmed, knives, hand tools.

*Close* — Same room or short corridor (~10m). Revolvers, shotguns, SMGs.

*Long* — Across a large open space (~50m). Pulse rifles, smart rifles, GPMGs.

*Extreme* — Very far (~100m+). Sniper rifles and heavy weapons.

Range is listed on each weapon's stat block. When in doubt: if you can throw something at it, it's Close. If you need a scope, it's Long or Extreme.

title: Death
body:
If death seems imminent, make your last moments count: save someone's life, solve a mystery, buy the others time to escape.

When you die, the Warden makes a *Death Save*: place 1d10 in a cup, shake it, set it face down on the table. As soon as someone spends a turn checking your vitals, the die is revealed and looked up on the *Death Table* (d10).

*Death Table:*
• *Roll 1 (00):* Unconscious. Wake in 2d10 min. Reduce Max Health by 1d5.
• *Roll 2–3 (01–02):* Unconscious and dying. Die in 1d5 rounds without intervention.
• *Roll 4–5 (03–04):* Comatose. Only extraordinary measures can return you.
• *Roll 6–10 (05–09):* You have died. Roll up a new character.

title: Atmospheres
body:
*Toxic Atmosphere:* Planet air is unbreathable but not corrosive. Requires a rebreather or armor with its own O2 supply. Without protection: *1d10 DMG per round, Body Save for half.*

*Corrosive Atmosphere:* Deals damage every round. Ranges from *1 DMG/round (Mildly Corrosive)* to *10 DMG/round (Highly Corrosive)*. Anything higher is impossible to traverse without specialised equipment.

title: Bleeding
body:
Some weapons and Wounds cause *Bleeding*: take *1 Damage every round* until stopped. This is cumulative — if already bleeding 1/round and gain *Bleeding +1*, now take 2/round.

Bleeding damage *ignores Armor and Damage Reduction.*

Stop bleeding with a First Aid Kit or a successful Field Medicine check.

title: Cryosickness
body:
After emerging from cryosleep, you experience a hangover-like state: *[-] on all rolls for 1 week.*

Upgraded cryochambers can mitigate these effects. A *Stimpak* cures cryosickness instantly.

title: Exhaustion
body:
After *12 hours of activity*, make a *Body Save every hour*. On failure: gain 1 Stress and take 1 Damage.

After *24 hours of exhaustion*: *[-] on all rolls* until you rest for 8 hours.

title: Food & Water
body:
*Food:* Humans survive ~3 weeks without food. After *24 hours without food*: *[-] on all rolls.*

*Water:* Minimum 1 litre/day. At this bare minimum, any strenuous activity (running, combat, repairs) requires a *Body Save or pass out.*

When water is being tracked closely: *[-] on all rolls.*

title: Oxygen
body:
In space: *15 seconds without O2 before falling unconscious.* After unconscious: *1d5 minutes before dying.*

If ship Life Support goes offline: roll *1d10 × max crew capacity* = remaining O2 supply. Subtract active crew count every 24 hours. Strenuous activity (combat, repairs) reduces O2 by *2 extra per person.*

• *O2 < 2× breathing crew:* [-] on all rolls (headaches, fatigue).
• *O2 < breathing crew:* Body Save or Death Save (dizziness, impaired vision).
• *O2 = 0:* 15 sec to unconscious, 1d5 min to death.

Androids and cryosleeping crew do not consume O2.

title: Radiation
body:
*Radiation Table:*
• *Level 1 — TRACE:* Cosmic rays, everyday sources. No immediate damage. Possible long-term effects (cancer).
• *Level 2 — ACUTE:* Unshielded reactors / Warp Cores. *Reduce all Stats and Saves by 1 every round.*
• *Level 3 — LETHAL:* Atomic weapons, direct Warp Core handling. Every round: *Body Save or lethal dose (death in 1d5 days).*

Armor with *Radiation Shielding* (e.g. Hazard Suit) blocks all three levels.

title: Stimpak Overdose
body:
Taking more than *one Stimpak per day* risks overdose.

Whenever a character takes more than 1 Stimpak in 24 hours: roll *1d10*. If you roll *under the number of doses taken in the past 24 hours* → make a *Death Save.*

title: Temperature
body:
In most climates temperature has no notable effect. In *extreme* conditions, make a *Body Save every hour* or succumb.

*Extreme Cold:* Hypothermia and frostbite set in within 10–30 min without proper gear. Can kill in 30 min to 6 hours.

*Extreme Heat (100°F / 40°C+):* Heat stroke can kill within hours. Victim must move to a cooler location immediately.

title: Short Term Recovery
body:
Once per day, after *6+ hours of rest*: make a *Body Save*. On success, reset Health to Maximum.

Wounds are *not* healed by rest — they require medical treatment.

title: Medical Treatments
body:
Recovering Wounds, Conditions, or lost Stats/Saves requires paid treatment:

• *Artificial Wellness Counselor* — 150cr. 1 hr session (max 1/wk). Restores 1 Sanity Save. 1% chance: random Condition.
• *Immersive Slicksim Therapy* — 1kcr. 4 hr virtual session. Restores 1d10 Combat or 1d10 Fear Save. 1% chance: stuck 1d10 days, lose 1d5 Sanity Save.
• *Deep Tissue Nanogel Massage* — 24kcr. 1 hr (max 1/wk). Reduces Minimum Stress by 1. [-] all actions 24 hrs.
• *Medpod* — 6kcr. 1 week in pod. Restores 1 Wound. Does not restore lost limbs/digits.
• *Cognitive Defragmentation* — 100kcr. 24 hr surgery. Removes 1 Condition. 1% chance: total amnesia. [-] on Intellect, Sanity, Fear for 4 weeks.
• *Pseudoflesh Injection* — 18kcr. 8 hr surgery. Restores 2d10 Speed, 2d10 Strength, 2d10 Body Save, or all Wounds. [-] all rolls 2 wks + 4 wks convalescence.
• *Psychosurgery* — 28kcr. 8 hr surgery. Restores Intellect, Sanity Save, or Fear Save to max, or reduces Min Stress to 2. [-] all rolls 4 weeks.

title: Cover
body:
The environment can provide protection called *Cover.* It can be destroyed, just like armor, whenever it is dealt Damage greater than or equal to its AP. Cover typically only protects against ranged attacks, but in some situations may help block a hand-to-hand attack. *If you shoot while in Cover, you are considered out of Cover until your next turn.*

*COVER TABLE*

• *INSIGNIFICANT COVER* — Wood furniture/doors, body shields, etc. | AP: 5
• *LIGHT COVER* — Trees, bulkhead walls, metal furniture, etc. | AP: 10
• *HEAVY COVER* — Airlock doors, cement beams, ships, etc. | DR: 5, AP: 20

title: Building Your Campaign
body:
A game of Mothership takes place over one or more *sessions* and is called a *campaign.* Many campaigns are a single session (a One Shot). Most are 5-7 sessions. Long campaigns can last years or be ongoing indefinitely.

*WHAT DO I NEED TO PREP?*
• A *starter scenario* to run first.
• A *campaign style* — how the game is broadly structured and what kind of play to expect.
• A *campaign frame* — who the players are and what kind of work they do.
• A *starting area* to play in.
• A few *tools* to maintain and grow the campaign over time.

*PITCHING THE GAME*
Once you have the style and frame worked out, pitch your concept to your players. This gets everyone on the same page.

💡 Being a Warden is a lot of work — and you are a player, too. Make sure the style and frame line up with your interests and the kind of prep you want to do.

title: Campaign Style
body:
Your *campaign style* describes broadly how the campaign is structured and the kind of game your players can expect. You can switch styles between campaigns, or even mid-campaign.

*ANTHOLOGY* — Sessions and adventures have little narrative cohesion. Each adventure stands alone.
*APOCALYPSE* — Play centers around a massive threat to life in the universe (alien invasion, interstellar war, plague, AI revolt).
*ENSEMBLE* — Players routinely control multiple characters and switch between them.
*EPISODIC* — Heavy emphasis on downtime and roleplaying, frequent time skips and slice-of-life encounters.
*HEROIC* — Players are key figures in a large-scale epic struggle with stakes affecting millions.
*NARRATIVE* — Players unravel a cohesive narrative as part of the game foreground or background.
*OPEN TABLE* — Features a large pool of potential players who may drop in or out each session.
*SANDBOX* — Features a large area with many potential leads the players can tackle in any order.
*SERIAL* — Play takes place from moment to moment, day to day, with infrequent time skips.

title: Starting Area & Jump Cluster
body:
Turn to a new spread in your *Campaign Notebook* and copy the map, leaving out all text. This becomes the starting area: a small Jump-1 cluster in rimspace.

*MAP LEGEND*
• *Solid Lines* — Jump-1 Routes between systems. Label each J1.
• *Dotted Lines* — Jump Route to/from the cluster. Roll 1d5+1 for the route number (higher = more remote).
• *Empty Circles* — An orbit showing the relationship between dots.
• *Filled Dots* — Each dot is a planet, moon, or space station.
• *Jump Points* — A dot connected to a Jump Route is the closest location to that Jump Point.

*KEYING THE MAP*
Number each dot, then key the map. Put the first scenario somewhere on the map. Fill in the key over time using scenarios you prep and official modules. Do not fill it all in at once — wait to see what your players care about. Write a scrap of an idea for each location, enough to say something is there, then move on.

💡 *Good prep is a terrible substitute for good play.*

*FINDING NEW JUMP ROUTES*
Only the most powerful corporations and governments have resources to explore hyperspace. Finding new routes can be a mission precursor or reward for a job well done. Always be adding to the map, growing it into a sprawling atlas.

title: Factions
body:
Players will interact with organizations, corporations, cults, gangs, and other groups — called *factions.* They operate as allies, enemies, and employers.

*CREATING A FACTION*
Title a new page in your Campaign Notebook with the faction's name, then note:
• *VIPs* — Important members with short descriptions and page references.
• *Locations* — Notable locations with map or scenario references.
• *Goals* — Compelling goals that will affect players if completed.

Factions become most useful once your players have encountered two or three. This creates situations where players must choose between competing interests.

*FACTION GOALS*
Assign each goal a number of boxes (1-2 for easy, 5-10 for difficult). Roll 1d100 at regular intervals that fit your game's pace:
• *Odds* — Faction makes progress. Fill in a box.
• *Evens* — Faction hits an obstacle. Mark a box with X.
• *Even doubles* — Breakthrough! Mark off two boxes (or fill one marked X).
• *Odd doubles* — Catastrophe. Erase a filled box.
• *All boxes filled* — Faction achieves its goal.

title: The Company
body:
The first faction to design is *the Company* — the interstellar megacorporation dominating the corporate-controlled future of Mothership.

The Company can serve as:
• *Primary employer or client* — Players have to work for someone, and the Company has the resources.
• *Principal antagonist* — A persistent, faceless, inexhaustible threat that keeps coming back.
• *Framing device* — Tells you what work players will do and what technologies foreground your setting.

*CORPORATE MIND MAP*
On a new page, write The Company in the center and circle it. Draw connections: important people, subsidiaries, secret projects, planets, ports, and enemies. Add to it as time goes on.

*CORPORATE POWER*
Assume functionally unlimited resources. Assume it is above the law. Assume it always has another crew — just as disposable, just as desperate — to clean up if your players cannot. Assume illegal research, regulatory violations, bribery, negligence, and embezzlement. Assume invasive biometric tracking, industrial sabotage, proxy wars, human experimentation, rampant pollution. Assume their products cause cancer. Assume they secretly fund terrorist groups. Assume they have done all of this before. *Assume they will never stop.*

title: Work & Job Board
body:
Keeping an updated *Job Board* ensures players always have new work. Create one with 1d5 jobs:

1. Title a new Campaign Notebook spread *Job Board.*
2. Create a table with headers: *Job, Client, Pay,* and *Location.*
3. Roll or choose from the *Contract Work Table.*
4. Roll or choose from the *Pay Scale Table* for each job.
5. Assign a faction as the client, with a specific contact if relevant.
6. Note where players can meet the client to start the job.

Prune and add entries over time. Start with one new job and one removed job per in-game year.

*NEGOTIATING RATES*
Adjust pay by 10% per factor:
• Crew's reliable/unreliable reputation.
• Previous good/bad work for this client.
• Client is desperate/has other options.
• Work is time-sensitive/dangerous.

Beyond that, the client walks away looking for other options.

title: Pay
body:
*SALARY*
Players working for the Company earn a monthly salary based on skills:
• *500cr/month* per Trained Skill.
• *1,000cr/month* per Expert Skill.
• *2,000cr/month* per Master Skill.

*JUMP PAY*
A flat bonus equal to Jump x 1,000cr, regardless of distance or time in hyperspace.

*HAZARD PAY*
During incredibly dangerous work: 1d5 months of salary as a bonus. This does not constitute an admission of liability on the part of the Company or its agents.

title: Campaign Economics & Debt
body:
Money in Mothership limits options — another resource like Health and Stress to manage, on a longer time scale.

• The shorter the campaign, the less important economics are.
• *Favors and information are more interesting rewards than credits.*
• *This is a horror game, not a small business simulator.*
• *Upper mobility is statistically impossible.* Wealth is concentrated in a few generationally wealthy companies and families.

*NET WORTH*
• *Hundreds* — Food, shelter.
• *Thousands* — Weapons, equipment, Shore Leave, rent, space travel.
• *Hundreds of thousands* — Cybermods, contractors, skill training, vehicles.
• *Millions* — Ship repairs, fuel, maintenance.
• *Tens of millions* — Mechs, small spacecraft, businesses.
• *Hundreds of millions* — Ships, asteroids, small fleets.
• *Billions* — Companies, moons, private armies.
• *Trillions* — War, colonization, planets, space stations.

*DEBT*
Debt means owing violent people a non-trivial amount of money. Increase Minimum Stress by 1 for every significant debtor owed.

*Securing a Loan:*
• Downpayment of 1d5x10% up front, or equivalent collateral.
• Interest equal to the amount borrowed — players pay back twice as much.
• Term: 2d10 months. Payments of 10% of total owed per month.

*Consequences for Non-Payment:*
Violent debt collectors, targeted malware, threats to allies, blacklisting, repossession, credstick freezes, kidnap and ransom, forced relocation to debtor colonies, or a bounty on the debtor's life.

title: Getting Rich
body:
*SAVING MONEY*
On a day-to-day basis, players save whatever salary they do not spend. With large time skips: players can save *one month's salary per year.* Saving extra requires raising Minimum Stress by the same amount.

*WHAT IF THEY GET RICH?*

1. *Big money attracts big predators.* Windfall gains paint a target on players' backs. Piracy increases, corrupt customs inspectors appear, enemies form alliances.

2. *Allies will suddenly need help.* Wealthy players attract desperate allies seeking emergency funding. Family and friends become kidnap-and-ransom targets.

3. *Old money hates new money.* High society does not welcome new members. Expect skeptics and scam artists.

4. *It's okay to retire. For now.* Megarich players may retire in style. The character operates as an ally or NPC — until called back for one last job.

*RETIREMENT COSTS*
• *1mcr* — Fixed income. Cramped homeworld. Part-time job.
• *10mcr* — Second home. Core world. S-Class vacations. Space travel.
• *100mcr* — Social elite. Multiple homes. Life of luxury.
• *1bcr* — Shareholder. Megayacht. Board seat. Private asteroid.
• *10bcr* — C-level ancestral home. Space station. Personal army.
• *100bcr* — Noble title. Company owner. Private planet. Personal fleet.

title: House Rules
body:
Common house rules that adjust difficulty up or down. Add any that work for your group to the *House Rules* page in your Campaign Notebook.

*ABLATIVE WOUNDS* — Players gain 1 extra Wound that, when lost, requires no Wound table roll. Regained with 30m rest.
*ARMOR DEGRADATION* — AP reduced by 1 whenever excess Damage is dealt. Destroyed at 0 AP.
*CRITICAL STRESS RELIEF* — Critical Success reduces Stress by 1.
*EXHAUSTABLE SKILLS* — Auto-succeed each Skill once per session.
*FRAGILITY* — All players get 1 Wound (androids get 5).
*HIGH SCORE BREAKER* — Beating your High Score grants 20 points to divide between Stats and Saves.
*IMPENETRABLE WOUNDS* — Damage does not carry over after a Wound is received.
*IMPROVED ADVANCEMENT* — Stats and Saves can both improve from Shore Leave.
*LETHALITY* — Ignore Health, use only Wounds. All weapons deal 1+ Wounds.
*LIGHT AMMO TRACKING* — Track ammo only when narratively relevant; then assume 1d5 shots remaining.
*OPT-IN STRESS* — Players volunteer to take Stress and make Panic Checks when appropriate.
*PLAYER FACING ROLLS* — Players make all rolls; failure in combat could mean being hit instead.
*RAPID SKILL LEARNING* — Trained in 3 sessions, Expert in 5, Master in 10.
*RESOLVE* — Each session survived grants 1 Resolve, spendable as a free reroll.
*SIMPLE SKILLS* — Ignore Skill bonuses; all Skills grant Advantage instead.
*SKILLS AS WISHLIST* — After session 1, note your players' skills. Treat this as a wishlist of what they want to see. Hit one or two each session.
